bugs.description: When extending a subsequence the code checks `if x < num:` where `x` is the XOR value, not the last element of the subsequence. It should compare the stored last element `dp_max[x]` with `num`. This allows invalid (non‑increasing) subsequences and produces incorrect XOR values.

The code inserts 0 into the result list unconditionally and then sorts again, causing a duplicate 0 when 0 is already present in `dp` (e.g., when a non‑empty increasing subsequence has XOR 0). This leads to an incorrect count and duplicate output.

When extending a subsequence the code checks `if x < num:` where `x` is the XOR value, not the last element of the subsequence. It should compare the stored last element `dp_max[x]` with `num`. This allows invalid (non‑increasing) subsequences and produces incorrect XOR values.

The code inserts 0 into the result list unconditionally and then sorts again, causing a duplicate 0 when 0 is already present in `dp` (e.g., when a non‑empty increasing subsequence has XOR 0). This leads to an incorrect count and duplicate output.

The condition `if x < num:` incorrectly compares the xor value `x` with the current number instead of the last element of the subsequence that produced `x`. It should compare `dp_max[x]` (the last element) with `num`. This allows extensions that are not strictly increasing in value, producing invalid xor results.

The DP keeps the maximal last element for each xor value, but to maximize future extensions it should keep the minimal possible last element. The updates use `<` where `>` is required (lines 18 and 22), causing the algorithm to discard a better (smaller) last element and miss reachable xor values.

The condition `if x < num:` incorrectly compares the xor value `x` with the current number instead of the last element of the subsequence that produced `x`. It should compare `dp_max[x]` (the last element) with `num`. This allows extensions that are not strictly increasing in value, producing invalid xor results.

The DP keeps the maximal last element for each xor value, but to maximize future extensions it should keep the minimal possible last element. The updates use `<` where `>` is required (lines 18 and 22), causing the algorithm to discard a better (smaller) last element and miss reachable xor values.

The program reads the array a from only a single line (line 5). If the input array spans multiple lines, which is allowed by the problem statement, the code will read an incomplete array and produce incorrect results.
